questions.stem: What does `printf("%d", 5 / 2);` print in C?
questions.explanation: Both `5` and `2` are integer literals, so `5 / 2` uses integer division, which discards the fractional part and gives `2`. To get `2.5` you would need a floating-point operand, for example `5.0 / 2`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: What does `printf("%d", 7 % 3);` print?
questions.explanation: The `%` operator gives the remainder of integer division. `7` divided by `3` is `2` with a remainder of `1`, so `7 % 3` is `1`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Which format specifier does `printf` use to print a value of type `int`?
questions.explanation: `%d` (or equivalently `%i`) prints a signed decimal integer. `%f` is for `float`/`double`, `%c` for a single character, and `%s` for a string.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Which format specifier should you use in `printf` to display a `double`?
questions.explanation: In `printf`, both `float` and `double` are printed with `%f` because a `float` argument is promoted to `double` when passed. Note `scanf` is different: it needs `%lf` to read a `double`. `%d` would misinterpret the bits as an integer.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: What does `printf("%c", 'A' + 1);` print? (ASCII of `'A'` is 65.)
questions.explanation: A `char` literal has an integer value, so `'A' + 1` is `65 + 1 = 66`, which is the ASCII code for `B`. Because `%c` prints the value as a character, the output is `B`. Using `%d` instead would have printed `66`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: What does `printf("%f", (float)7 / 2);` print?
questions.explanation: The cast `(float)7` turns `7` into `7.0` before the division, so `7.0 / 2` is floating-point division giving `3.5`. With `%f` the default is six decimal places, so it prints `3.500000`. Without the cast, `7 / 2` would be integer division giving `3`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: How do you declare a constant named `PI` with value `3.14159` that cannot be reassigned?
questions.explanation: The `const` qualifier makes a variable read-only after initialization, and `double` holds the fractional value. `int` would truncate `3.14159` to `3`, and `:=` and `constant` are not C syntax.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Which preprocessor directive defines a symbolic constant `MAX` equal to `100`?
questions.explanation: `#define MAX 100` tells the preprocessor to replace every `MAX` token with `100` before compilation. There is no `=` and no trailing `;` (a `;` would become part of the replacement text). It must begin with `#`, and `#const` is not a directive.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: What does `printf("%d", (int)3.99);` print?
questions.explanation: Casting a floating-point value to `int` truncates toward zero, simply dropping the fractional part. It does not round, so `(int)3.99` is `3`, not `4`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Which `scanf` call correctly reads an integer into a variable declared as `int n;`?
questions.explanation: `scanf` needs the address of the variable so it can store the value there, so you pass `&n`. Passing `n` by itself sends only its value, which `scanf` cannot write back to. `%f` is the wrong specifier for an `int`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: What is the value of `x` after `int x = 10; x += 5;`?
questions.explanation: The compound assignment `x += 5` is shorthand for `x = x + 5`. Starting from `10`, adding `5` gives `15`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: After `int a = 5; int b = a++;`, what are the values of `a` and `b`?
questions.explanation: The post-increment `a++` yields the old value of `a` (which is `5`) for the assignment to `b`, and only afterward increments `a` to `6`. So `b` becomes `5` and `a` becomes `6`. Pre-increment `++a` would instead give `b` the value `6`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: What does `printf("%d", 10 > 3);` print? (A C relational expression yields an `int`.)
questions.explanation: In C a relational comparison evaluates to the `int` value `1` when true and `0` when false. Since `10 > 3` is true, it prints `1`. C has no built-in `true` keyword printed by `%d`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Which line correctly declares an integer variable and gives it the value `42`?
questions.explanation: A C declaration is `type name = value;`. The keyword for whole numbers is `int`, not `integer`. A single `=` assigns; `==` is the equality test and would not initialize the variable.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: What does `printf("%d", 2 + 3 * 4);` print? (`*` binds tighter than `+`.)
questions.explanation: Multiplication has higher precedence than addition, so `3 * 4` is evaluated first to get `12`, then `2 + 12` gives `14`. The answer `20` would require `(2 + 3) * 4`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: What does `printf("%d", 5 / 2 * 2);` print? (Evaluation is left to right for equal precedence.)
questions.explanation: `/` and `*` share precedence and group left to right, so `5 / 2` is computed first as integer division giving `2`, then `2 * 2` gives `4`. The intermediate truncation is why the result is not `5`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Which data type is most appropriate for storing a single character such as `'x'`?
questions.explanation: The `char` type holds a single character (one byte). C has no built-in `string` type; a string is an array of `char`. While a `char` is technically a small integer, `char` is the type that expresses the intent of one character.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: What does `printf("%d", sizeof(char));` print on a standard C implementation?
questions.explanation: By definition in C, `sizeof(char)` is always `1`. A `char` is the unit that `sizeof` measures everything else against. An `int` is commonly `4` bytes, but a `char` is `1`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem:
What does this program print?

`int a = 9, b = 4;`
`printf("%d", a - b);`
questions.explanation: Both are integers, so `a - b` is `9 - 4 = 5`, printed with `%d` as `5`. Multiple variables can be declared in one statement separated by commas.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: In the assignment `double y = 5;`, what value is stored in `y`?
questions.explanation: Assigning the integer literal `5` to a `double` performs an implicit conversion, so `y` holds the floating-point value `5.0`. Printing it with `%f` would show `5.000000`. This is a widening conversion and is allowed without a cast.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: What does `printf("%d-%d", 5, 10);` print?
questions.explanation: Each `%d` is replaced in order by the matching argument, and the literal `-` between the two specifiers is printed as-is. So the output is `5-10`. `printf` does not sum the values or change the separator.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: What does `printf("%.2f", 3.14159);` print?
questions.explanation: The `.2` in `%.2f` sets the precision to two digits after the decimal point, and the value is rounded to that precision, giving `3.14`. Three decimals (`%.3f`) would give `3.142`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: What does `printf("%d", -7 / 2);` print in standard C?
questions.explanation: Integer division in C truncates toward zero (not toward minus infinity). So `-7 / 2` is `-3.5` truncated toward zero, which is `-3`. Truncating toward minus infinity would have given `-4`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Which escape sequence inside a `printf` string moves output to a new line?
questions.explanation: `\n` is the newline escape sequence. `\t` inserts a horizontal tab, `\0` is the null character that terminates strings, and `\\` produces a single literal backslash.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Predict the output of integer arithmetic and type casting in C.
questions.solution: Given `int a = 17, b = 5;`, the expression `a / b` evaluates to {{1}} and `a % b` evaluates to {{2}}. To get the true decimal quotient `3.4`, you would write `(double)a / b`, and the matching `printf` specifier for that `double` result is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Integer division `17 / 5` truncates to `3`, and the remainder `17 % 5` is `2` (since `5 * 3 = 15` and `17 - 15 = 2`). Casting one operand with `(double)a` forces floating-point division, and a `double` is printed with `%f`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Choose the correct declaration and I/O calls for reading a number.
questions.solution: To store a whole number that can be reassigned, the keyword is {{1}}. To read such a value typed by the user into a variable `n`, the correct call is {{2}}. To then print it back, the format specifier for an integer is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: `int` declares a reassignable integer. `scanf` must receive the address `&n` to write into it, so `scanf("%d", &n);` is correct. The integer is displayed with `%d`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Predict outputs that depend on operator precedence and casting.
questions.solution: The expression `2 + 6 / 3` evaluates to {{1}} because division happens before addition. The expression `(2 + 6) / 3` evaluates to {{2}} using integer division. Casting with `(int)4.7` produces {{3}} because the cast truncates rather than rounds.
questions.explanation: Without parentheses, `6 / 3 = 2` is computed first, then `2 + 2 = 4`. With parentheses, `(2 + 6) = 8`, then `8 / 3` truncates to `2`. The cast `(int)4.7` drops the fraction to give `4`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Fill in the constants and character arithmetic.
questions.solution: The preprocessor directive that defines a symbolic constant `LIMIT` of `50` is `#define LIMIT` {{1}} (no equals sign, no semicolon). Separately, since `'A'` has ASCII code `65`, the integer value of the expression `'C' - 'A'` is {{2}}, and the `printf` specifier that would display a single character is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: A `#define` is written `#define LIMIT 50` with no `=` and no `;`. Character literals carry their ASCII values, so `'C' - 'A'` is `67 - 65 = 2`. A single character prints with `%c`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Predict the printed values and the floating-point format.
questions.solution: The statement `printf("%d", 9 % 4);` prints {{1}}. The statement `printf("%d", 8 % 4);` prints {{2}}. The statement `printf("%.1f", 2.0 / 4);` prints the value {{3}} to one decimal place.
questions.explanation: `9 % 4` leaves remainder `1` (since `4 * 2 = 8`). `8 % 4` divides evenly, so the remainder is `0`. Because `2.0` is a `double`, `2.0 / 4` is floating-point division giving `0.5`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Identify the right types for scientific data.
questions.solution: To store a temperature like `36.6` with fractional precision, a suitable type is {{1}}. To store the count of measurements taken (a whole number), the natural type is {{2}}. The value of `sizeof(char)` in C is always {{3}}.
questions.explanation: A fractional measurement needs a floating-point type such as `double`. A whole count fits an `int`. By the C standard, `sizeof(char)` is defined to be `1`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Read two integers from input and print their sum.
questions.explanation: Read the two values with one scanf and print their sum. `scanf("%d %d", &a, &b)` skips the spaces between the numbers for you.
questions.source: in the style of the KNUST Scientific Computing course (Introduction to C, MATH 279 / HPC)

questions.stem: Read three integers u, a and t and print the value of v = u + a*t (final velocity, initial velocity plus acceleration times time).
questions.explanation: This is the kinematics formula from the slides. Read the three values and print `u + a*t`. For 10, 2, 5 the result is 10 + 10 = 20.
questions.source: in the style of the KNUST Scientific Computing course (Introduction to C, MATH 279 / HPC)

questions.stem: Read three integers P, R and T and print the simple interest, P*R*T/100. Assume the result is a whole number.
questions.explanation: The simple interest formula from the slides is P*R*T/100. For P = 1000, R = 5, T = 2 the interest is 10000/100 = 100.
questions.source: in the style of the KNUST Scientific Computing course (Introduction to C, MATH 279 / HPC)

questions.stem: Read an integer and print it with its digits reversed, for example 123 becomes 321.
questions.explanation: Peel the last digit with `% 10` and build the reversed number by multiplying the running result by 10 and adding it. A trailing zero in the input just disappears.
questions.source: in the style of the KNUST Scientific Computing course (Introduction to C, MATH 279 / HPC)

questions.stem: Read a non-negative integer and print its binary representation (base 2), with no leading zeros.
questions.explanation: Divide by 2 repeatedly; the remainders are the binary digits from least to most significant. Printing them on the way back out of the recursion puts them in the right order.
questions.source: in the style of the KNUST Scientific Computing course (Introduction to C, MATH 279 / HPC)
